category.text: $course$/CSIR NET Mathematics PYQ/Unit 1 - Analysis/Part B - Real Analysis

name.text: PYQ Part B - Cauchy Sequence in R

questiontext.text:
[CSIR NET | Part B | 3 Marks]

A sequence \((x_n)\) in \(\mathbb{R}\) is a Cauchy sequence if and only if it:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B)

Since \(\mathbb{R}\) is complete, a sequence in \(\mathbb{R}\) is Cauchy if and only if it converges. Option (A) is wrong: bounded sequences need not converge (e.g., \((-1)^n\)). Option (C) is wrong: \(|x_{n+1}-x_n|\to 0\) does not imply Cauchy (e.g., harmonic partial sums). Option (D) is a consequence, not a characterisation.

name.text: PYQ Part B - Limit (1+1/n)^n

questiontext.text:
[CSIR NET | Part B | 3 Marks]

\(\displaystyle\lim_{n \to \infty}\left(1 + \frac{1}{n}\right)^n\) equals:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C)

This is the definition of Euler's number \(e \approx 2.718\). The sequence is strictly increasing and bounded above, and converges to \(e\). It can be shown using the binomial theorem or the definition of \(e\) as \(\lim_{n\to\infty}(1+1/n)^n\).

name.text: PYQ Part B - Telescoping Series Sum

questiontext.text:
[CSIR NET | Part B | 3 Marks]

The sum of the series \(\displaystyle\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \frac{1}{n(n+1)}\) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B)

Use partial fractions: \(\frac{1}{n(n+1)} = \frac{1}{n} - \frac{1}{n+1}\). The partial sum is \(S_N = 1 - \frac{1}{N+1} \to 1\) as \(N\to\infty\).

name.text: PYQ Part B - Alternating Series ln 2

questiontext.text:
[CSIR NET | Part B | 3 Marks]

The series \(\displaystyle\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \frac{(-1)^{n+1}}{n}\) converges to:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A)

From the Taylor series \(\ln(1+x) = x - \frac{x^2}{2} + \frac{x^3}{3} - \cdots\) valid for \(x \in (-1,1]\), substituting \(x = 1\) gives \(\ln 2 = 1 - \frac{1}{2} + \frac{1}{3} - \cdots = \sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\frac{(-1)^{n+1}}{n}\). Convergence at \(x=1\) follows from Abel's theorem.

name.text: PYQ Part B - Limsup of (-1)^n(1+1/n)

questiontext.text:
[CSIR NET | Part B | 3 Marks]

\(\displaystyle\limsup_{n \to \infty}\,(-1)^n\!\left(1 + \frac{1}{n}\right)\) equals:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C)

For even \(n\): \((-1)^n(1+1/n) = 1+1/n \to 1^+\). For odd \(n\): the terms approach \(-1\). The \(\limsup\) is the largest subsequential limit, which is \(1\) (achieved along even-\(n\) subsequence).

name.text: PYQ Part B - p-Series Convergence

questiontext.text:
[CSIR NET | Part B | 3 Marks]

The series \(\displaystyle\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \frac{1}{n^p}\) converges if and only if:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B)

The \(p\)-series \(\sum 1/n^p\) converges for \(p > 1\) and diverges for \(p \leq 1\). This follows from the integral test: \(\int_1^\infty x^{-p}\,dx\) converges iff \(p > 1\). In particular, the harmonic series (\(p=1\)) diverges.

name.text: PYQ Part B - Radius of Convergence of Sum n! x^n

questiontext.text:
[CSIR NET | Part B | 3 Marks]

The radius of convergence of the power series \(\displaystyle\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} n!\, x^n\) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A)

By the ratio test: \(\left|\frac{a_{n+1}}{a_n}\right| = (n+1)|x| \to \infty\) for any \(x \neq 0\). So the series diverges for every \(x \neq 0\), giving radius of convergence \(R = 0\).

name.text: PYQ Part B - Uniform Continuity on Compact Set

questiontext.text:
[CSIR NET | Part B | 3 Marks]

Let \(f : [0,1] \to \mathbb{R}\) be continuous. Which of the following is necessarily true?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A)

By the Heine-Cantor theorem, a continuous function on a compact set is uniformly continuous. Since \([0,1]\) is compact, \(f\) must be uniformly continuous. Continuity does not imply differentiability, monotonicity, or that \(f\) assumes only finitely many values.

name.text: PYQ Part B - Additive Continuous Function

questiontext.text:
[CSIR NET | Part B | 3 Marks]

Let \(f : \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}\) be continuous and satisfy \(f(x+y) = f(x) + f(y)\) for all \(x, y \in \mathbb{R}\). Then \(f(x)\) must equal:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B)

From \(f(x+y)=f(x)+f(y)\), one deduces \(f(n)=nf(1)\) for integers, then for rationals, and by continuity for all reals. Setting \(c = f(1)\) gives \(f(x) = cx\).

name.text: PYQ Part B - Derivative of x^2 sin(1/x) at 0

questiontext.text:
[CSIR NET | Part B | 3 Marks]

Let \(f(x) = x^2 \sin\!\left(\dfrac{1}{x}\right)\) for \(x \neq 0\) and \(f(0) = 0\). Then \(f'(0)\) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (D)

By definition: \(f'(0) = \lim_{h\to 0}\frac{f(h)-f(0)}{h} = \lim_{h\to 0} h\sin(1/h)\). Since \(|\sin(1/h)| \leq 1\), we have \(|h\sin(1/h)| \leq |h| \to 0\). Hence \(f'(0) = 0\). Note that \(f'\) is not continuous at \(0\).

name.text: PYQ Part B - Pointwise Limit of x^n on [0,1]

questiontext.text:
[CSIR NET | Part B | 3 Marks]

The sequence \(f_n(x) = x^n\) on \([0,1]\) converges:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B)

For \(x \in [0,1)\): \(x^n \to 0\). At \(x=1\): \(1^n = 1 \to 1\). So the pointwise limit is \(f(x) = 0\) for \(x \in [0,1)\) and \(f(1)=1\). The convergence is NOT uniform because \(f\) is discontinuous while each \(f_n\) is continuous.

answer.text: Pointwise to \(0\) on \([0,1)\) and to \(1\) at \(x=1\), but not uniformly
Does not converge at any point

name.text: PYQ Part B - Cantor Set Properties

questiontext.text:
[CSIR NET | Part B | 3 Marks]

The Cantor (middle-thirds) set \(C \subset [0,1]\) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C)

The Cantor set \(C\) has the cardinality of \(\mathbb{R}\) (it is uncountable — in bijection with \(\{0,2\}^{\mathbb{N}}\)) yet has Lebesgue measure zero (the total length removed is \(\sum_{n=0}^\infty 2^n/3^{n+1} = 1\)). It is closed and nowhere dense (contains no open interval).

name.text: PYQ Part B - Riemann Integrability Criterion

questiontext.text:
[CSIR NET | Part B | 3 Marks]

A bounded function \(f:[a,b]\to\mathbb{R}\) is Riemann integrable if and only if:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (D)

Lebesgue's criterion: a bounded function on \([a,b]\) is Riemann integrable iff the set of its discontinuities has Lebesgue measure zero. Continuity everywhere is sufficient but not necessary (e.g., the function \(f=1\) on rationals, \(0\) elsewhere, has measure-zero discontinuity set vacuously — wait, that one is NOT Riemann integrable). Actually: monotone functions (finitely many discontinuities) and piecewise continuous functions are Riemann integrable. The exact characterisation is measure-zero discontinuity set.

answer.text: \(f\) has at most finitely many discontinuities
The set of discontinuities of \(f\) has Lebesgue measure zero

name.text: PYQ Part B - Improper Integral 1/sqrt(x)

questiontext.text:
[CSIR NET | Part B | 3 Marks]

The improper integral \(\displaystyle\int_0^1 \frac{1}{\sqrt{x}}\,dx\) equals:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C)

\(\int_0^1 x^{-1/2}dx = \left[2x^{1/2}\right]_0^1 = 2(1) - 2(0) = 2\). The integral converges because \(p = 1/2 < 1\) in \(\int_0^1 x^{-p}dx\), which converges for \(p < 1\).

name.text: PYQ Part B - Weierstrass M-Test

questiontext.text:
[CSIR NET | Part B | 3 Marks]

The Weierstrass M-test guarantees uniform convergence of \(\sum f_n\) on a set \(E\) if there exist constants \(M_n\) such that:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A)

The Weierstrass M-test: if \(|f_n(x)| \leq M_n\) for all \(x \in E\) and \(\sum M_n < \infty\), then \(\sum f_n\) converges absolutely and uniformly on \(E\). This is one of the most used tools to establish uniform convergence of series of functions.

answer.text: \(|f_n(x)| \leq M_n\) for all \(x \in E\) and \(\sum M_n < \infty\)

name.text: PYQ Part B - Limit n^(1/n)

questiontext.text:
[CSIR NET | Part B | 3 Marks]

\(\displaystyle\lim_{n \to \infty} n^{1/n}\) equals:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B)

Let \(a_n = n^{1/n}\). Then \(\ln a_n = \frac{\ln n}{n} \to 0\) as \(n\to\infty\) (by L'Hôpital or squeeze). Hence \(a_n = e^{\ln a_n} \to e^0 = 1\).

name.text: PYQ Part B - Bolzano-Weierstrass Theorem

questiontext.text:
[CSIR NET | Part B | 3 Marks]

The Bolzano-Weierstrass theorem states that every bounded sequence in \(\mathbb{R}\):

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B)

Every bounded sequence in \(\mathbb{R}\) has a convergent subsequence — this is the Bolzano-Weierstrass theorem. It does NOT assert that the sequence itself converges (e.g., \((-1)^n\) is bounded but diverges). It also does not assert a unique subsequential limit.

name.text: PYQ Part B - Mean Value Theorem Application

questiontext.text:
[CSIR NET | Part B | 3 Marks]

Let \(f\) be continuous on \([a,b]\) and differentiable on \((a,b)\) with \(f'(x) = 0\) for all \(x \in (a,b)\). Then:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A)

By the Mean Value Theorem, for any \(x, y \in [a,b]\), there exists \(c\) between them with \(f(x)-f(y) = f'(c)(x-y) = 0\). Hence \(f(x)=f(y)\) for all \(x,y\), i.e., \(f\) is constant on \([a,b]\).

name.text: PYQ Part B - Supremum of S = {1-1/n}

questiontext.text:
[CSIR NET | Part B | 3 Marks]

Let \(S = \!\left\{1 - \dfrac{1}{n} : n \in \mathbb{N}\right\}\). Then \(\sup S\) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C)

Every element \(1 - 1/n < 1\), so \(1\) is an upper bound. For any \(\epsilon > 0\), choose \(n > 1/\epsilon\) so that \(1 - 1/n > 1 - \epsilon\). Hence \(1\) is the least upper bound. Note \(\sup S = 1 \notin S\), so the maximum does not exist.

name.text: PYQ Part B - Absolute Convergence Implies Convergence of Squares

questiontext.text:
[CSIR NET | Part B | 3 Marks]

If \(\displaystyle\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} a_n\) converges absolutely, then \(\displaystyle\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} a_n^2\):

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C)

Absolute convergence of \(\sum a_n\) implies \(a_n \to 0\), so eventually \(|a_n| < 1\), giving \(a_n^2 < |a_n|\). By comparison with \(\sum |a_n| < \infty\), the series \(\sum a_n^2\) converges absolutely. The converse is false: \(\sum 1/n^2\) converges but \(\sum 1/n\) diverges.

category.text: $course$/CSIR NET Mathematics PYQ/Unit 1 - Analysis/Part B - Linear Algebra

name.text: PYQ Part B - Rank-Nullity Theorem

questiontext.text:
[CSIR NET | Part B | 3 Marks]

If \(A\) is an \(m \times n\) matrix with \(\text{rank}(A) = r\), the dimension of the null space of \(A\) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B)

By the Rank-Nullity Theorem: \(\text{rank}(A) + \text{nullity}(A) = n\) (number of columns). Hence \(\text{nullity}(A) = n - r\). Note it is \(n - r\), not \(m - r\).

name.text: PYQ Part B - Determinant of Product

questiontext.text:
[CSIR NET | Part B | 3 Marks]

For \(n \times n\) matrices \(A\) and \(B\), \(\det(AB)\) equals:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B)

The determinant is multiplicative: \(\det(AB) = \det(A)\det(B)\). This follows from the fact that det is the unique alternating multilinear form normalised to 1 on \(I\). Note \(\det(AB) = \det(BA)\) but \(\det(A+B) \neq \det A + \det B\) in general.

name.text: PYQ Part B - Trace Equals Sum of Eigenvalues

questiontext.text:
[CSIR NET | Part B | 3 Marks]

For an \(n \times n\) matrix \(A\) with eigenvalues \(\lambda_1, \lambda_2, \ldots, \lambda_n\) (counting multiplicity), \(\text{tr}(A)\) equals:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A)

The trace equals the sum of eigenvalues: \(\text{tr}(A) = \sum_{i=1}^n \lambda_i\), and \(\det(A) = \prod_{i=1}^n \lambda_i\). These follow by comparing coefficients in the characteristic polynomial \(\det(\lambda I - A)\).

name.text: PYQ Part B - Eigenvalues of Real Symmetric Matrix

questiontext.text:
[CSIR NET | Part B | 3 Marks]

Let \(A\) be a real symmetric matrix (\(A^T = A\)). Then all eigenvalues of \(A\) are:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B)

If \(Av = \lambda v\) with \(v \neq 0\), then \(\lambda \|v\|^2 = v^T A v = (Av)^T v = \bar{\lambda}\|v\|^2\) (using \(A=A^T=\bar{A}\)), so \(\lambda = \bar{\lambda}\), meaning \(\lambda\) is real. They need not be positive or negative definite.

name.text: PYQ Part B - Cayley-Hamilton Theorem

questiontext.text:
[CSIR NET | Part B | 3 Marks]

The Cayley-Hamilton theorem states that for any \(n \times n\) matrix \(A\) with characteristic polynomial \(p(\lambda) = \det(\lambda I - A)\):

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C)

Cayley-Hamilton: every matrix satisfies its own characteristic polynomial, i.e., \(p(A) = 0\). For example, if \(p(\lambda) = \lambda^2 - 5\lambda + 6\), then \(A^2 - 5A + 6I = 0\). This is useful for computing matrix inverses and powers.

name.text: PYQ Part B - Eigenvalues of Skew-Symmetric Matrix

questiontext.text:
[CSIR NET | Part B | 3 Marks]

If \(A\) is a real skew-symmetric matrix (\(A^T = -A\)), the eigenvalues of \(A\) are:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (D)

If \(Av = \lambda v\), then \(\lambda\|v\|^2 = v^T Av = -v^T A^T v = -(Av)^T v = -\bar\lambda\|v\|^2\), so \(\lambda = -\bar\lambda\), i.e., \(\text{Re}(\lambda) = 0\). The eigenvalues are purely imaginary or zero.

name.text: PYQ Part B - Orthogonal Matrix Determinant

questiontext.text:
[CSIR NET | Part B | 3 Marks]

If \(Q\) is a real orthogonal matrix (\(Q^T Q = I\)), then \(|\det(Q)|\) equals:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B)

From \(Q^TQ = I\): \(\det(Q^T)\det(Q) = 1\), and \(\det(Q^T) = \det(Q)\), so \((\det Q)^2 = 1\), giving \(\det Q = \pm 1\). Hence \(|\det Q| = 1\). Orthogonal matrices represent rotations (\(\det = +1\)) or reflections (\(\det = -1\)).

name.text: PYQ Part B - Eigenvalues of Idempotent Matrix

questiontext.text:
[CSIR NET | Part B | 3 Marks]

If \(A\) is an \(n \times n\) idempotent matrix (\(A^2 = A\)), the possible eigenvalues of \(A\) are:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C)

If \(\lambda\) is an eigenvalue with eigenvector \(v\): \(A^2 v = A(Av) = A(\lambda v) = \lambda^2 v\). But \(A^2 = A\), so \(A^2 v = Av = \lambda v\). Hence \(\lambda^2 = \lambda\), giving \(\lambda(\lambda-1)=0\), so \(\lambda \in \{0,1\}\).

name.text: PYQ Part B - Eigenvalues of Nilpotent Matrix

questiontext.text:
[CSIR NET | Part B | 3 Marks]

If \(A\) is an \(n \times n\) nilpotent matrix (\(A^k = 0\) for some positive integer \(k\)), then all eigenvalues of \(A\) are:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A)

If \(Av = \lambda v\), then \(A^k v = \lambda^k v = 0\), so \(\lambda^k = 0\), hence \(\lambda = 0\). All eigenvalues of a nilpotent matrix are zero. Equivalently, the characteristic polynomial of an \(n\times n\) nilpotent is \(\lambda^n\).

name.text: PYQ Part B - Rank of Product AB

questiontext.text:
[CSIR NET | Part B | 3 Marks]

For matrices \(A_{m\times n}\) and \(B_{n\times p}\), which inequality holds?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B)

\(\text{rank}(AB) \leq \min(\text{rank}(A),\,\text{rank}(B))\). The column space of \(AB\) is contained in the column space of \(A\), so rank\((AB) \leq\) rank\((A)\). Similarly rank\((AB) \leq\) rank\((B)\).

name.text: PYQ Part B - Eigenvalues of Transpose

questiontext.text:
[CSIR NET | Part B | 3 Marks]

The eigenvalues of \(A^T\) compared to the eigenvalues of \(A\) are:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A)

\(\det(\lambda I - A^T) = \det((\lambda I - A)^T) = \det(\lambda I - A)\). So \(A\) and \(A^T\) have the same characteristic polynomial, hence the same eigenvalues (with multiplicities). Their eigenvectors may differ.

name.text: PYQ Part B - Similar Matrices Same Characteristic Polynomial

questiontext.text:
[CSIR NET | Part B | 3 Marks]

If \(B = P^{-1}AP\) for some invertible \(P\), then \(A\) and \(B\):

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B)

\(\det(\lambda I - B) = \det(\lambda I - P^{-1}AP) = \det(P^{-1}(\lambda I - A)P) = \det(\lambda I - A)\). So similar matrices share the same characteristic polynomial (and hence the same eigenvalues, trace, and determinant), but generally have different column spaces and null spaces.

name.text: PYQ Part B - Nullity of 4x4 rank-3 Matrix

questiontext.text:
[CSIR NET | Part B | 3 Marks]

Let \(A\) be a \(4 \times 4\) matrix with \(\text{rank}(A) = 3\). The dimension of the solution space of \(Ax = 0\) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B)

By the Rank-Nullity Theorem: nullity\((A) = n - \text{rank}(A) = 4 - 3 = 1\). So the homogeneous system \(Ax = 0\) has a one-dimensional solution space (one free variable).

name.text: PYQ Part B - Matrix A^T A is Positive Semidefinite

questiontext.text:
[CSIR NET | Part B | 3 Marks]

For any real matrix \(A\), the matrix \(A^T A\) is always:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B)

For any vector \(x\): \(x^T(A^TA)x = (Ax)^T(Ax) = \|Ax\|^2 \geq 0\). So \(A^TA\) is positive semidefinite. It is positive definite iff \(A\) has full column rank. It is always symmetric: \((A^TA)^T = A^TA\).

category.text: $course$/CSIR NET Mathematics PYQ/Unit 1 - Analysis/Part C - Real Analysis

name.text: PYQ Part C - Baire Category Theorem

questiontext.text:
[CSIR NET | Part C | 4.75 Marks]

The Baire Category Theorem states that a complete metric space \((X, d)\):

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B)

Baire Category Theorem: a complete metric space (or a locally compact Hausdorff space) is of the second category, i.e., it cannot be written as a countable union of nowhere-dense sets. Equivalently, a countable intersection of open dense sets is dense. This is used to prove the existence of continuous nowhere-differentiable functions, etc.

answer.text: Cannot be written as a countable union of nowhere-dense sets
Every Cauchy sequence has a unique limit

name.text: PYQ Part C - Non-negative Continuous Function with Zero Integral

questiontext.text:
[CSIR NET | Part C | 4.75 Marks]

Let \(f : [0,1] \to \mathbb{R}\) be continuous with \(f(x) \geq 0\) for all \(x \in [0,1]\), and \(\displaystyle\int_0^1 f(x)\,dx = 0\). Then:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A)

Suppose \(f(c) > 0\) for some \(c\). By continuity, \(f > f(c)/2 > 0\) on some interval \((c-\delta, c+\delta)\). Then \(\int_0^1 f \geq \int_{c-\delta}^{c+\delta} f \geq f(c)/2 \cdot 2\delta > 0\), contradiction. Hence \(f \equiv 0\).

answer.text: \(f\) can be positive at isolated points

name.text: PYQ Part C - Arzela-Ascoli Theorem

questiontext.text:
[CSIR NET | Part C | 4.75 Marks]

The Arzela-Ascoli theorem guarantees that a sequence \(\{f_n\}\) of functions on a compact interval has a uniformly convergent subsequence if \(\{f_n\}\) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B)

Arzela-Ascoli: a subset of \(C(K)\) (continuous functions on a compact set \(K\)) is relatively compact (every sequence has a uniformly convergent subsequence) if and only if it is uniformly bounded and equicontinuous. Pointwise boundedness alone is insufficient.

name.text: PYQ Part C - Gaussian Integral

questiontext.text:
[CSIR NET | Part C | 4.75 Marks]

The value of \(\displaystyle\int_0^{\infty} e^{-x^2}\,dx\) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C)

Let \(I = \int_0^\infty e^{-x^2}dx\). Then \(I^2 = \int_0^\infty\!\int_0^\infty e^{-(x^2+y^2)}dx\,dy\). Converting to polar: \(I^2 = \int_0^{\pi/2}\!\int_0^\infty e^{-r^2}r\,dr\,d\theta = \frac{\pi}{2}\cdot\frac{1}{2} = \frac{\pi}{4}\). Hence \(I = \frac{\sqrt{\pi}}{2}\). Also \(\Gamma(1/2) = \sqrt{\pi}\), so \(I = \frac{1}{2}\Gamma(1/2)\).

name.text: PYQ Part C - fn = n^2 x e^(-nx) Convergence

questiontext.text:
[CSIR NET | Part C | 4.75 Marks]

Let \(f_n(x) = n^2 x\, e^{-nx}\) on \([0,1]\). Which statement is correct?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B)

For each fixed \(x > 0\): \(f_n(x) = n^2 x e^{-nx} \to 0\) (exponential beats polynomial). At \(x=0\): \(f_n(0)=0\). So \(f_n \to 0\) pointwise. But \(\int_0^1 f_n\,dx = \int_0^1 n^2 x e^{-nx}dx\). Substituting \(u=nx\): \(= \int_0^n u e^{-u}du \to \int_0^\infty u e^{-u}du = \Gamma(2) = 1 \neq 0\). So the limit of integrals \(\neq\) integral of limit: convergence is NOT uniform.

answer.text: \(f_n\) does not converge at any point

name.text: PYQ Part C - Uniform Convergence and Integration

questiontext.text:
[CSIR NET | Part C | 4.75 Marks]

If \(f_n \to f\) uniformly on \([a,b]\) and each \(f_n\) is Riemann integrable on \([a,b]\), then:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B)

Uniform convergence preserves Riemann integrability: \(f\) is Riemann integrable and \(\int_a^b f_n\,dx \to \int_a^b f\,dx\). This is a key theorem — pointwise convergence alone does NOT allow interchange of limit and integral. Uniform convergence also preserves continuity but not necessarily differentiability.

answer.text: \(f\) is Riemann integrable and \(\int_a^b f_n\,dx \to \int_a^b f\,dx\)

name.text: PYQ Part C - Completeness of C([0,1]) with Sup Norm

questiontext.text:
[CSIR NET | Part C | 4.75 Marks]

The space \(C([0,1])\) of continuous real-valued functions on \([0,1]\), equipped with the supremum norm \(\|f\|_\infty = \sup_{x\in[0,1]}|f(x)|\), is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B)

\(C([0,1])\) with \(\|\cdot\|_\infty\) is a Banach space (complete normed space). Completeness: if \(\{f_n\}\) is Cauchy in \(\|\cdot\|_\infty\), it converges uniformly, and the uniform limit of continuous functions is continuous. It is NOT a Hilbert space (inner product would give \(L^2\)-norm, not sup-norm). It is infinite-dimensional and not compact.

name.text: PYQ Part C - Lebesgue Dominated Convergence

questiontext.text:
[CSIR NET | Part C | 4.75 Marks]

Let \(\{f_n\}\) be a sequence of measurable functions with \(f_n \to f\) a.e. and \(|f_n(x)| \leq g(x)\) for all \(n\), where \(g\) is Lebesgue integrable. Then by the Dominated Convergence Theorem:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A)

The Lebesgue Dominated Convergence Theorem (DCT): under the stated conditions, \(f\) is integrable and \(\lim_{n\to\infty}\int f_n = \int f\). The dominating function \(g\) is essential: without it, the conclusion may fail (as seen in the \(f_n = n^2 xe^{-nx}\) example).

answer.text: \(\int f_n\) is bounded but need not converge

name.text: PYQ Part C - Banach Fixed Point Theorem

questiontext.text:
[CSIR NET | Part C | 4.75 Marks]

Let \((X, d)\) be a complete metric space and \(T : X \to X\) a contraction mapping, i.e., \(d(Tx, Ty) \leq k\,d(x,y)\) for some \(k \in [0,1)\) and all \(x, y \in X\). Then:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C)

Banach Fixed Point Theorem (Contraction Mapping Principle): \(T\) has a unique fixed point \(x^* \in X\) (i.e., \(Tx^* = x^*\)), and the iterates \(T^n(x_0) \to x^*\) for any starting point \(x_0\). The completeness of \(X\) and the contraction condition \(k < 1\) are both essential.

answer.text: \(T\) has at least two fixed points

name.text: PYQ Part C - Cesaro Mean Convergence

questiontext.text:
[CSIR NET | Part C | 4.75 Marks]

If a sequence \((x_n)\) satisfies \(x_n \to L\), and \(\sigma_n = \dfrac{1}{n}\displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^n x_k\) denotes the Cesaro mean, then:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B)

If \(x_n \to L\), then \(\sigma_n \to L\) (Cesaro means converge to the same limit). However, the converse is false: \(\sigma_n \to L\) does not imply \(x_n \to L\) (e.g., \(x_n = (-1)^n\) has \(\sigma_n \to 0\) but \(x_n\) diverges). This is Cesaro summability.

name.text: PYQ Part C - Beta Function

questiontext.text:
[CSIR NET | Part C | 4.75 Marks]

The Beta function \(\displaystyle B(m,n) = \int_0^1 x^{m-1}(1-x)^{n-1}\,dx\) for \(m, n > 0\) equals:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B)

\(B(m,n) = \dfrac{\Gamma(m)\Gamma(n)}{\Gamma(m+n)}\). This follows from the relation \(B(m,n) = 2\int_0^{\pi/2}\cos^{2m-1}\theta\sin^{2n-1}\theta\,d\theta\) and properties of the Gamma function. Special cases: \(B(1,1)=1\), \(B(1/2,1/2)=\pi\).

name.text: PYQ Part C - Limsup Definition

questiontext.text:
[CSIR NET | Part C | 4.75 Marks]

For a bounded sequence \((x_n)\) in \(\mathbb{R}\), \(\displaystyle\limsup_{n\to\infty} x_n\) equals:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B)

\(\limsup_{n\to\infty} x_n = \inf_{n \geq 1}\sup_{k \geq n} x_k\). Equivalently, it is the largest subsequential limit of \((x_n)\). The sequence \(y_n = \sup_{k\geq n}x_k\) is decreasing and bounded below, so its limit is the \(\limsup\).

name.text: PYQ Part C - Stone-Weierstrass Density of Polynomials

questiontext.text:
[CSIR NET | Part C | 4.75 Marks]

By the Stone-Weierstrass theorem, polynomials are:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B)

The Weierstrass Approximation Theorem (a special case of Stone-Weierstrass): for every \(f \in C([a,b])\) and \(\epsilon > 0\), there exists a polynomial \(p\) with \(\|f - p\|_\infty < \epsilon\). Polynomials are dense in \(C([0,1])\) w.r.t. \(\|\cdot\|_\infty\). They are also dense in \(L^2([0,1])\), but the sup-norm statement is what Weierstrass provides directly.

answer.text: Dense in \(C([0,1])\) with respect to the supremum norm

name.text: PYQ Part C - Bounded Derivative Implies Uniform Continuity

questiontext.text:
[CSIR NET | Part C | 4.75 Marks]

Let \(f : \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}\) be differentiable with \(|f'(x)| \leq M\) for all \(x \in \mathbb{R}\). Then \(f\) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B)

By the MVT: \(|f(x)-f(y)| = |f'(c)||x-y| \leq M|x-y|\). This is the Lipschitz condition with constant \(M\), which implies uniform continuity (for any \(\epsilon > 0\), take \(\delta = \epsilon/M\)). \(f\) need not be bounded (e.g., \(f(x)=x\)).

category.text: $course$/CSIR NET Mathematics PYQ/Unit 1 - Analysis/Part C - Linear Algebra

name.text: PYQ Part C - Diagonalizability via Geometric Multiplicity

questiontext.text:
[CSIR NET | Part C | 4.75 Marks]

An \(n \times n\) matrix \(A\) is diagonalizable over its base field if and only if:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B)

A matrix is diagonalizable iff for each eigenvalue \(\lambda\), the geometric multiplicity (dimension of eigenspace \(\ker(A-\lambda I)\)) equals the algebraic multiplicity (multiplicity as a root of the characteristic polynomial). Having \(n\) distinct eigenvalues is sufficient but not necessary (e.g., \(I_n\) is diagonalizable with one eigenvalue of multiplicity \(n\)).

answer.text: For each eigenvalue, geometric multiplicity equals algebraic multiplicity

name.text: PYQ Part C - Minimal Polynomial Divides Characteristic Polynomial

questiontext.text:
[CSIR NET | Part C | 4.75 Marks]

Let \(m(\lambda)\) be the minimal polynomial and \(p(\lambda)\) the characteristic polynomial of an \(n\times n\) matrix \(A\). Then:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B)

The minimal polynomial \(m\) divides the characteristic polynomial \(p\) (by Cayley-Hamilton: \(p(A) = 0\), and \(m\) is the monic polynomial of least degree annihilating \(A\)). Also, every root of \(m\) is a root of \(p\), and vice versa (they have the same roots, but possibly different multiplicities). The degree of \(m\) is at most \(n\).

name.text: PYQ Part C - Spectral Theorem for Symmetric Matrices

questiontext.text:
[CSIR NET | Part C | 4.75 Marks]

By the Spectral Theorem, every real symmetric \(n\times n\) matrix \(A\):

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B)

Spectral Theorem: every real symmetric matrix \(A\) can be orthogonally diagonalised: \(A = Q\Lambda Q^T\) where \(Q\) is orthogonal and \(\Lambda\) is diagonal with real eigenvalues. Eigenvectors for distinct eigenvalues are orthogonal. This is the most important structural theorem in linear algebra for symmetric matrices.

answer.text: Can be written as \(A = Q\Lambda Q^T\) where \(Q\) is orthogonal and \(\Lambda\) is real diagonal

name.text: PYQ Part C - Positive Definite Matrix Characterization

questiontext.text:
[CSIR NET | Part C | 4.75 Marks]

A real symmetric matrix \(A\) is positive definite if and only if:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B)

A real symmetric matrix \(A\) is positive definite iff all eigenvalues are positive (by Spectral Theorem). Equivalent conditions: all leading principal minors are positive (Sylvester's criterion); \(x^TAx > 0\) for all \(x \neq 0\). Having all diagonal entries positive is necessary but not sufficient.

name.text: PYQ Part C - Jordan Form Already in Jordan Form

questiontext.text:
[CSIR NET | Part C | 4.75 Marks]

The Jordan normal form of \(A = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 2 & 1 \\ 0 & 0 & 2 \end{pmatrix}\) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A)

\(A\) is already in Jordan normal form — it is a single \(3\times 3\) Jordan block with eigenvalue \(2\). Its characteristic polynomial is \((\lambda-2)^3\) and minimal polynomial is also \((\lambda-2)^3\). Since \(A\) is not diagonalizable (\(\ker(A-2I)\) is 1-dimensional), it cannot be diagonalized to \(\text{diag}(2,2,2)\).

name.text: PYQ Part C - Singular Values of a Matrix

questiontext.text:
[CSIR NET | Part C | 4.75 Marks]

The singular values of an \(m \times n\) real matrix \(A\) are defined as:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B)

The singular values of \(A\) are the non-negative square roots of the eigenvalues of \(A^T A\) (equivalently, of \(AA^T\)). They are always real and non-negative. The SVD writes \(A = U\Sigma V^T\) where \(U, V\) are orthogonal and \(\Sigma\) is diagonal with singular values on the diagonal.

answer.text: The non-negative square roots of eigenvalues of \(A^T A\)
The absolute values of eigenvalues of \(A\)

name.text: PYQ Part C - Rank-1 Matrix Eigenvalues

questiontext.text:
[CSIR NET | Part C | 4.75 Marks]

Let \(A\) be an \(n \times n\) real matrix with \(\text{rank}(A) = 1\). Then \(A\) has:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B)

If rank\((A)=1\) then \(A = uv^T\) for some column vectors \(u, v \in \mathbb{R}^n\). The null space has dimension \(n-1\), so eigenvalue \(0\) has geometric multiplicity \(n-1\) (hence algebraic multiplicity at least \(n-1\)). The only possibly non-zero eigenvalue is \(\text{tr}(A) = v^Tu\) (the remaining eigenvalue).

answer.text: Eigenvalue \(0\) with algebraic multiplicity at least \(n-1\)

name.text: PYQ Part C - Normal Matrix Orthogonal Diagonalizability

questiontext.text:
[CSIR NET | Part C | 4.75 Marks]

A real \(n \times n\) matrix \(A\) is orthogonally diagonalizable if and only if:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A)

A real matrix is orthogonally diagonalizable iff it is symmetric (\(A = A^T\)). For complex matrices, the analogous statement is: unitarily diagonalizable iff normal (\(AA^* = A^*A\)). Real symmetric matrices are a special case of normal matrices. A real normal matrix that is not symmetric is NOT orthogonally diagonalizable (it may have complex eigenvalues).
